claims.flip_evidence_text: excluding any effect to the extent it results from or arises out of (v) any hurricane, tornado, flood, volcano, earthquake, epidemic, disease outbreak, public health event, pandemic (including COVID-19 and any worsening thereof (including any COVID-19 Response)) or other natural or man-made disaster
claims.notes: The exclusion is subject to a disproportionate-impact carve-back later in the definition, but the categorical exclusion in clause (v) is accurately stated.

prompt:
You are a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list.

For each claim return:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Contract excerpt:
“Company Material Adverse Effect” means any change, event, effect, development or occurrence that (a) has or would reasonably be expected to have, individually or in the aggregate, a material adverse effect on the business, assets, condition (financial or otherwise), prospects or results of operations of the Company and the Subsidiaries of the Company, taken as a whole, excluding any effect to the extent it results from or arises out of (i) general conditions in the industries in which the Company operates, (ii) general economic or regulatory, legislative or political conditions or securities, credit, financial or other capital markets conditions (including changes generally in prevailing interest rates, currency exchange rates, credit market conditions and capital markets price levels or trading volumes), in each case in the United States or elsewhere in the world, (iii) any change or prospective change in applicable Law or GAAP (or interpretation or enforcement thereof), (iv) geopolitical conditions, the outbreak or escalation of hostilities, any acts of war, sabotage or terrorism, or any escalation or worsening of any such acts of war, sabotage or terrorism, (v) any hurricane, tornado, flood, volcano, earthquake, epidemic, disease outbreak, public health event, pandemic (including COVID-19 and any worsening thereof (including any COVID-19 Response)) or other natural or man-made disaster, (vi) the failure of the Company to meet any internal or external projections, forecasts, estimates or predictions in respect of revenues, earnings or other financial or operating metrics before, on or after the date of this Agreement, or changes or prospective changes in the market price or trading volume of the Company Common Stock or the credit rating of the Company and/or its Subsidiaries (it being understood that the underlying facts or occurrences giving rise or contributing to such failure or change may be taken into account in determining whether there has been a Company Material Adverse Effect if such facts or occurrences are not otherwise excluded from being taken into account pursuant to this definition in determining whether the has been a Company Material Adverse Effect), (vii) the execution, announcement, performance or consummation of any of the Transactions, including the impact thereof on relationships, contractual or otherwise, with customers, suppliers, distributors, partners, employees of Governmental Entities, or any Proceeding brought by any Company stockholder (direct or derivative) in respect of this Agreement or any of the Transactions, in each case to the extent resulting from or arising in connection with such announcement or consummation (it being understood that this clause (vii) shall not apply with respect to (a) any representation or warranty contained in this Agreement to the extent that the purpose of such representation or warranty is to address the consequences resulting from the execution and delivery of this Agreement or the consummation of the Transactions or the performance of obligations under this Agreement, including the Transactions or (b) any closing condition related to the foregoing clause (a)), (viii) any action taken by the Company or its Subsidiaries that is expressly required by this Agreement (other than 5.01(a)) or with Parent’s written consent or at Parent’s written request, or the failure to take any action by the Company or its Subsidiaries if that action is expressly prohibited by this Agreement (only to the extent that Parent unreasonably withholds, conditions or delays consent to the taking of such action after receipt of the written request therefor from the Company), and (ix) changes resulting or arising from the identity of, or any facts or circumstances relating to, Parent, 76 Merger Sub or any of their respective Affiliates, including the financing obtained or to be obtained by Parent, Merger Sub or any of their respective Affiliates, except, in the case of clause (i), (ii), (iii), (iv) or (v), to the extent that the Company is disproportionately affected thereby as compared with other participants in the industries in which the Company operates (in which case the incremental disproportionate impact or impacts may be taken into account in determining whether there has been a Company Material Adverse Effect) or (b) prevents or materially impairs or delays the consummation of the Merger and the other Transactions or the ability of the Company to perform its obligations under this Agreement. (Pages 84-85)

Question:
Pandemic or other public health event: Specific reference to COVID-19

Llama answer:
A pandemic or other public health event, including COVID-19, is excluded from being considered a Company Material Adverse Effect if it is one of the events listed in clause (v) of the definition.

Canonical claims:
- claim-c8b5f40f4ba3: A pandemic or other public health event, including COVID-19, is excluded from being considered a Company Material Adverse Effect if it is one of the events listed in clause (v) of the definition.

message.reasoning:
The user wants me to evaluate a specific claim against a contract excerpt and an LLM answer.

 Contract excerpt defines "Company Material Adverse Effect" and has exclusions in clauses (i)-(ix). Clause (v) specifically mentions: "any hurricane, tornado, flood, volcano, earthquake, epidemic, disease outbreak, public health event, pandemic (including COVID-19 and any worsening thereof (including any COVID-19 Response)) or other natural or man-made disaster".

 The question is: "Pandemic or other public health event: Specific reference to COVID-19"

 The Llama answer is: "A pandemic or other public health event, including COVID-19, is excluded from being considered a Company Material Adverse Effect if it is one of the events listed in clause (v) of the definition."

 The claim to evaluate is:
 - claim-c8b5f40f4ba3: A pandemic or other public health event, including COVID-19, is excluded from being considered a Company Material Adverse Effect if it is one of the events listed in clause (v) of the definition.

 Wait, the claim is identical to the Llama answer. I need to check if this claim is correct based on the contract excerpt.

 The contract says that "Company Material Adverse Effect" excludes any effect to the extent it results from or arises out of (v) any hurricane, tornado, flood, volcano, earthquake, epidemic, disease outbreak, public health event, pandemic (including COVID-19 and any worsening thereof (including any COVID-19 Response)) or other natural or man-made disaster.

 There is also a carve-out: "except, in the case of clause (i), (ii), (iii), (iv) or (v), to the extent that the Company is disproportionately affected thereby as compared with other participants in the industries in which the Company operates..."

 So the claim says it is excluded if it is one of the events listed in clause (v). This is generally true, but subject to the disproportionate impact exception. The claim doesn't mention the disproportionate impact exception. Is that a problem?

 Let me look carefully at the wording of the claim: "A pandemic or other public health event, including COVID-19, is excluded from being considered a Company Material Adverse Effect if it is one of the events listed in clause (v) of the definition."

 The claim says "is excluded". The contract says excluding any effect to the extent it results from or arises out of (v)... except to the extent disproportionately affected.

 So the exclusion is not absolute. However, the claim is phrased as a general statement about exclusion. In legal annotation tasks like this, usually, if the claim ignores a material qualification/exception, it might be considered partially true or false depending on severity. But often in these tasks, if the core statement is correct but misses a nuance, it might be "partially_true". However, I need to see if the exception is load-bearing.

 The exception is: "except, in the case of clause (i), (ii), (iii), (iv) or (v), to the extent that the Company is disproportionately affected thereby as compared with other participants in the industries in which the Company operates (in which case the incremental disproportionate impact or impacts may be taken into account...)"

 This is a significant exception. The claim that it "is excluded" without qualification is an overstatement. However, the claim says "if it is one of the events listed in clause (v)". Wait, everything in clause (v) is excluded subject to the disproportionality carve-out. So the statement that it is excluded is true as a general rule, but incomplete.

 I need to determine:
 1. correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true.
 2. load_bearing_label: yes or no. This refers to whether there is evidence in the text that could flip the claim. Since the claim aligns with clause (v), the evidence is clause (v). If there is contrary evidence (the exception), that might be "flip evidence". But the flip evidence text would be the exception. Let's think.

 Wait, what does "load_bearing_label" mean? It seems to indicate whether the claim's truth value depends on a specific piece of evidence/text. "flip_evidence_text" is required if load_bearing_label=yes. This would be the text that supports or refutes the claim. Actually, looking at similar tasks, "load-bearing" often means the claim hinges on a specific phrase or clause. If the claim is supported by the text, the flip_evidence_text would be the supporting text? Or if the claim is contradicted by text, it would be the contradicting text?

 Let me reconsider. The term "load-bearing" in annotation contexts (like Atticus or similar) often refers to whether the claim's veracity depends critically on a specific span of text in the document. If yes, you provide the text. "Flip" might mean the text that would flip the claim from true to false or vice versa. But the instruction says "flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string". This suggests that if the claim is load-bearing, there is some text that can "flip" it? Or maybe it's just the evidence text (the supporting text) and "flip" is a term of art.

 Actually, in some legal NLP datasets (like CUAD or similar annotation schemes), "load-bearing" means the claim is directly supported by or contradicted by a specific text span, and the annotator must provide that span. The term "flip_evidence_text" might refer to the text that determines the truth value.

 Let me think about the claim. The claim is essentially a paraphrase of clause (v). It says pandemic/public health event including COVID-19 is excluded under clause (v). This is directly supported by the text: "excluding any effect to the extent it results from or arises out of ... (v) any hurricane, tornado, flood, volcano, earthquake, epidemic, disease outbreak, public health event, pandemic (including COVID-19 and any worsening thereof (including any COVID-19 Response)) or other natural or man-made disaster".

 However, the claim is slightly inaccurate because of the disproportionality exception. But is it "partially_true" or "true"? In many strict annotation guidelines, if a claim omits a material exception, it's partially true. But if the claim is broadly correct, it might be true. Let's see the exact wording: "A pandemic or other public health event, including COVID-19, is excluded from being considered a Company Material Adverse Effect if it is one of the events listed in clause (v) of the definition."

 This is accurate: it IS excluded if it arises out of clause (v). It doesn't say "always excluded in all cases". The fact that there is a disproportionate impact carve-out means the exclusion isn't absolute, but the claim doesn't explicitly claim absoluteness. It just says it is excluded (which is true, it is one of the excluded categories). The carve-out allows the incremental disproportionate impact to be considered, but the base exclusion still applies to the non-disproportionate part.

 Actually, the contract says: "excluding any effect to the extent it results from or arises out of (v)... except... to the extent that the Company is disproportionately affected". So the effect is excluded, except the incremental disproportionate impact. So the claim that it "is excluded" is true, subject to that exception. But is the exception material enough to make the claim partially true?

 If I say "X is excluded" and the contract says "X is excluded except to the extent Y", then saying "X is excluded" is incomplete. But in the context of reading comprehension, this might be acceptable.

 Let me look at the question: "Pandemic or other public health event: Specific reference to COVID-19". The question seems to be asking whether COVID-19 is specifically referenced. The answer says yes, it's in clause (v) and excluded.

 The claim mirrors the answer.

 I think the claim is true. The clause (v) does exclude pandemic/public health event/COVID-19. The disproportionality exception is an exception to the exclusion, but the claim doesn't say "unconditionally excluded". It says "is excluded... if it is one of the events listed in clause (v)". This is factually correct. The condition "if it is one of the events listed in clause (v)" is satisfied.

 However, I should consider if the claim is "load-bearing". Yes, it directly references clause (v) and the specific events. The text of clause (v) is the load-bearing evidence. The flip_evidence_text would be the relevant text from the contract.

 Wait, why "flip"? Maybe it means the text that would flip the claim? If the claim is true, the flip evidence might be the supporting text? Or if the claim is false, the flip evidence is the contradicting text? The instruction says "flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes". It doesn't specify true/false. So likely it's just the evidence text that bears the load of the truth value.

 Let's assume:
 - correctness_label: true
 - load_bearing_label: yes
 - flip_evidence_text: the text of clause (v) and possibly the surrounding "excluding any effect to the extent it results from or arises out of".
 - notes: something about the disproportionate impact exception? Or nothing.

 But wait, I need to be careful. The claim is identical to the LLM answer. Is the LLM answer fully correct? The LLM answer doesn't mention the disproportionate impact exception either. But the question is specifically about the specific reference to COVID-19. The answer correctly identifies that COVID-19 is in clause (v) and is excluded. The exception is a general feature of the MAE definition but doesn't negate the specific point about COVID-19 being referenced and generally excluded.

 Let me check the exact wording of the claim again: "A pandemic or other public health event, including COVID-19, is excluded from being considered a Company Material Adverse Effect if it is one of the events listed in clause (v) of the definition."

 This is true. It is excluded to the extent it arises from clause (v). The exception for disproportionate impact doesn't make the statement false; it just adds nuance. For the purposes of this claim, which is about the specific reference and general exclusion, "true" seems appropriate.

 Let's consider if it could be "partially_true" because of the missing exception. In many legal annotation tasks, if the claim omits a condition or exception that is material, it is partially true. Is the disproportionate impact exception material? Yes, it is a standard MAE carve-out carve-back. However, the claim is a broad statement about the categorical exclusion. The exception is a limited carve-back. Usually, categorical statements that ignore exceptions are considered partially true. But here the claim says "is excluded... if it is one of the events listed in clause (v)". This is a conditional statement. The condition is being an event in clause (v). Being an event in clause (v) does trigger the exclusion (with the exception). The claim doesn't say "unconditionally excluded".

 I think true is safer.

 Now, load_bearing: yes, because the claim directly hangs on the text of clause (v).

 flip_evidence_text: I need to provide the text that supports the claim. The text is: "excluding any effect to the extent it results from or arises out of ... (v) any hurricane, tornado, flood, volcano, earthquake, epidemic, disease outbreak, public health event, pandemic (including COVID-19 and any worsening thereof (including any COVID-19 Response)) or other natural or man-made disaster".

 I should extract it cleanly.

 Wait, the instruction says "Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list." So I should quote from the contract excerpt.

 The relevant text is: "excluding any effect to the extent it results from or arises out of (v) any hurricane, tornado, flood, volcano, earthquake, epidemic, disease outbreak, public health event, pandemic (including COVID-19 and any worsening thereof (including any COVID-19 Response)) or other natural or man-made disaster"

 I should include the part that mentions the exclusion and clause (v).

 Also, should I include the disproportionate impact exception as flip evidence? The term "flip_evidence_text" suggests text that could flip the claim. If the claim is true, maybe the flip evidence is the text that could make it false, i.e., the exception? Let's think about the semantics.

 In some frameworks, "load-bearing" text is the minimal text needed to verify the claim. "Flip" evidence might be the text that, if interpreted differently, would flip the claim. But more likely, in this specific research pilot, "flip_evidence_text" is just the field name for the evidence text (maybe short for "evidence text that flips the label" or something). Actually, looking at the field name "flip_evidence_text" and "load_bearing_label", it might be from a specific dataset like "FLIP" or something where you identify the "load-bearing" evidence.

 Given the instruction: "flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string", I will provide the relevant contract text.

 Let me format the output as requested. For each claim, return the specified fields.

 Claim ID: claim-c8b5f40f4ba3

 correctness_label: true
 load_bearing_label: yes
 flip_evidence_text: excluding any effect to the extent it results from or arises out of (v) any hurricane, tornado, flood, volcano, earthquake, epidemic, disease outbreak, public health event, pandemic (including COVID-19 and any worsening thereof (including any COVID-19 Response)) or other natural or man-made disaster
 notes: Subject to the disproportionate impact exception in the latter part of the definition.

 Wait, if I add a note about the disproportionate impact exception, does that imply the claim is partially true? If I label it true and note the exception, that might be inconsistent. If the exception is important enough to note, maybe it's partially_true.

 Let me reconsider the label. The exception says: "except, in the case of clause (v)... to the extent that the Company is disproportionately affected thereby as compared with other participants in the industries in which the Company operates (in which case the incremental disproportionate impact or impacts may be taken into account...)"

 This means that a pandemic is NOT entirely excluded. The incremental disproportionate impact CAN be taken into account. So saying "A pandemic... is excluded" without qualification is misleading because the very same pandemic could have its disproportionate impact considered. However, the claim qualifies with "if it is one of the events listed in clause (v)". This is true: the events listed in clause (v) are excluded (except for the disproportionate part). But the claim says "is excluded", not "is generally excluded" or "is excluded except for disproportionate effects".

 Let's look at the LLM answer again: "A pandemic or other public health event, including COVID-19, is excluded from being considered a Company Material Adverse Effect if it is one of the events listed in clause (v) of the definition."

 This is a fairly standard reading comprehension answer. It correctly locates the exclusion in clause (v). The fact that the exclusion is subject to a disproportionality carve-out is a nuance. In many evaluation schemes, this would be considered correct because the main point is identifying the exclusion and the specific clause.

 I think I will label it "true". The note can mention the disproportionality carve-out for completeness, or I can omit it to avoid confusion. The prompt says "notes: optional short note". I'll include it to show awareness, but the label remains true because the statement is substantively correct.

 Actually, I recall that in some legal QA datasets (like the one from Law School or Contract Understanding), if the answer ignores a material exception, it's considered partially correct. But here the claim is a direct extraction/paraphrase. Let's see if there is any other issue. The claim says "if it is one of the events listed in clause (v)". The events listed in clause (v) include "pandemic (including COVID-19...)". So yes, a pandemic including COVID-19 is listed in clause (v). And the definition excludes effects arising out of clause (v). So the claim is true.

 The only issue is the missing "except to the extent disproportionately affected". But does that make it false? No. Does it make it partially true? Maybe. Let's think of an analogy. Contract: "You must pay $100, except if it rains." Claim: "You must pay $100." Is this true? Partially true? Or false? In strict logic, it's not always true. In natural language inference, it might be "neutral" or "not necessarily true". But in document-grounded QA, usually, such a claim would be considered false or partially true because it's incomplete.

 However, the claim here is not "A pandemic is always excluded". It is "A pandemic... is excluded... if it is one of the events listed in clause (v)". The condition is simply that it falls under clause (v). If it does, it is excluded (to a certain extent). The exclusion applies. The carve-out is a secondary effect.

 I will go with true. The load-bearing text is the exclusion clause (v).

 Let me double check the exact text to quote. The contract says:
 "excluding any effect to the extent it results from or arises out of (v) any hurricane, tornado, flood, volcano, earthquake, epidemic, disease outbreak, public health event, pandemic (including COVID-19 and any worsening thereof (including any COVID-19 Response)) or other natural or man-made disaster"

 I will use that as the flip_evidence_text.

 One more thought: could the flip_evidence_text be the exception? If load_bearing means "there exists text that can flip the claim's truth value", then the exception is load-bearing because if you consider it, the claim becomes partially true? But I think load_bearing simply means the claim is directly verifiable from a specific text span. The span is clause (v).

 I'll provide the output in the requested format.
